steps.text: Ensure all persons entering the laboratory are advised of the potential hazards and meet specific entry/exit requirements.
Provide all persons who enter operational laboratory areas information on signs and symptoms of disease and appropriate occupational medical services.
Ensure laboratory personnel demonstrate proficiency in standard microbiological practices and techniques for working with agents requiring BSL-3 containment.
Establish a system for reporting and documenting near misses, laboratory accidents, exposures, and potential laboratory-associated illnesses.
Immediately evaluate incidents that result in exposure to infectious materials per institutional policy and report them to the laboratory supervisor and management.
Substitute plasticware for glassware whenever possible.
Limit the use of needles and syringes or other sharp instruments in the animal facility to situations where there is no alternative.
Perform uncapping of needles in a manner to reduce the potential for recoil causing an accidental needlestick.
Do not bend, shear, break, recap, remove needles from disposable syringes, or otherwise manipulate by hand before disposal.
If absolutely necessary to remove a needle from a syringe or recap a needle, use a hands-free device or comparable safety procedure.
Carefully place used, disposable needles and syringes in puncture-resistant containers for sharps disposal immediately after use.
Place non-disposable sharps in a hard-walled container for transport to a processing area for decontamination.
Do not handle broken glassware directly; remove using a brush and dustpan, tongs, or forceps.
Perform all procedures to minimize the creation of aerosols or splatters of infectious materials and waste.
Remove broken glassware using a brush and dustpan, tongs, or forceps.
Decontaminate work surfaces after completion of work and after any spill or splash of potentially infectious material with appropriate disinfectant.
Decontaminate all potentially infectious materials before transport or disposal using an effective method.
Contain, decontaminate, and clean up spills involving infectious materials by properly trained and equipped staff.
Ensure a spill procedure is developed and posted within the animal facility.
Decontaminate all cultures, stocks, and other potentially infectious materials before disposal using an effective method.
Place materials to be decontaminated outside of the immediate animal room in a durable, leak-proof container and secure for transport.
Disinfect the outer surface of the container with infectious materials prior to moving.
Pack materials to be removed from the facility for decontamination in accordance with applicable local, state, and federal regulations.
Ensure animals and plants not associated with the work are not permitted in areas where infectious materials and/or animals are housed or manipulated.
Provide animal care staff with information on signs and symptoms of disease, and medical services including evaluation, surveillance, treatment, and immunizations.
Ensure that testing systems developed and used for each of the tests performed in the laboratory provide quality laboratory services for all aspects of test performance.

Ensure that the physical plant and environmental conditions of the laboratory are appropriate for the testing performed and provide a safe environment in which employees are protected from physical, chemical, and biological hazards.

preconditions.text: Laboratory leadership should not conduct risk assessments alone, but should depend on the knowledge and expertise of the laboratory, infection prevention, and safety professionals.
Risk assessments should be documented and routinely evaluated, particularly when new instruments, tests, staff, or processes have been added to the laboratory environment.
Risk assessments should be evaluated when unanticipated or unusual events, near-misses, incidents, or accidents occur.

constraints.text: Typhoid fever is a demonstrated hazard to laboratory personnel and students working with S. Typhi.
Ingestion and, less frequently, parenteral inoculation are the most significant modes of transmission in the laboratory.
Handle ricin as a non-volatile toxic chemical.
Wear laboratory coat, gloves, and eye protection when handling ricin toxin.
Wear a full-face respirator if there is a potential for creating a toxin aerosol.
Use a BSC if there is any chance that ricin aerosols will be generated.
